coredata.dc:title: Urchin-like cobalt hydroxide coupled with N-doped carbon dots hybrid for enhanced electrocatalytic water oxidation

coredata.dc:description: The electrocatalytic water splitting into oxygen (O2) and hydrogen (H2) is one of promising technique for addressing the problem of energy shortage. Owing to the sluggish reaction kinetics for oxygen evolution reaction (OER), it is urgent to explore efficient and inexpensive OER electrocatalytic materials. Herein, a highly efficient three-dimensional (3D) hybrid of anions (Cl− and CO3 2−) doped cobalt hydroxide catalyst coupled with nitrogen-doped carbon dots (Co(OH)2@NCDs) is reported via a simple in situ hydrothermal method. Interestingly, the additional mass ratio of NCDs in Co(OH)2 hybrid has strong influence on the morphology of as-prepared composites. The well-defined urchin-like Co(OH)2@NCDs composite exhibits superior OER performance, achieving the low overpotential (η) of 296 mV at 10 mA cm−2 and high durability in 1 M KOH solution. The urchin-like Co(OH)2@NCDs catalyst could achieve 29-fold electrochemical active surface area than that of the pure Co(OH)2. Additionally, after coupling with NCDs, analysis result shows new Co-N charge transfer channel is generated in hybrid and an optimized electronic environment is originated from the strong electronic interaction among Co atom and NCDs, which can provide efficient charge channels for the rapid electron transfer and further improve the intrinsic activity and promote the OER kinetics. The work may endow a new strategy for the construction of excellent transition metal-based hydroxide water oxidation electrocatalysts.

(a) OER polarization curves of the various catalysts measured in N2-saturated 1.0 M KOH solution; (b) Corresponding magnifying LSV curves and (c) Tafel plots. (d) OER performance parameters of 0: Co(OH)2, 1: Co(OH)2@NCDs-1, 2: Co(OH)2@NCDs-2, 3: Co(OH)2@NCDs-3, 4: Co(OH)2@NCDs-4, 5: Co(OH)2@NCDs-5, and 6: RuO2 composites, respectively. (e) Plots applied to calculate the the C dl and further to represent ECSA of Co(OH)2, Co(OH)2@NCDs and RuO2 catalysts. (f) Nyquist plots for different samples at the modified GCE.

(a) Long-time stability test of Co(OH)2@NCDs-4 and RuO2 at a constant current density of 10 mA cm−2 for 20,000 s. (b) OER LSV curves of Co(OH)2@NCDs-4 before (black line) and after (red line) 2000 CV cycles test with a scan rate of 100 mV s−1.

head.ce:title: Urchin-like cobalt hydroxide coupled with N-doped carbon dots hybrid for enhanced electrocatalytic water oxidation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cobalt hydroxide coupled with N-doped carbon dots composites with different morphologies are controlled fabricated by a in situ hydrothermal method. The well-defined sea urchin-like Co(OH)2@NCDs hybrid shows the supreme oxidation evolution reaction (OER) performance in alkaline condition with lowest overpotential (296 mV at j = 10 mA cm−2) and nearly 100% Faradaic efficiency.

The electrocatalytic water splitting into oxygen (O2) and hydrogen (H2) is one of promising technique for addressing the problem of energy shortage. Owing to the sluggish reaction kinetics for oxygen evolution reaction (OER), it is urgent to explore efficient and inexpensive OER electrocatalytic materials. Herein, a highly efficient three-dimensional (3D) hybrid of anions (Cl− and CO3 2−) doped cobalt hydroxide catalyst coupled with nitrogen-doped carbon dots (Co(OH)2@NCDs) is reported via a simple in situ hydrothermal method. Interestingly, the additional mass ratio of NCDs in Co(OH)2 hybrid has strong influence on the morphology of as-prepared composites. The well-defined urchin-like Co(OH)2@NCDs composite exhibits superior OER performance, achieving the low overpotential (η) of 296 mV at 10 mA cm−2 and high durability in 1 M KOH solution. The urchin-like Co(OH)2@NCDs catalyst could achieve 29-fold electrochemical active surface area than that of the pure Co(OH)2. Additionally, after coupling with NCDs, analysis result shows new Co-N charge transfer channel is generated in hybrid and an optimized electronic environment is originated from the strong electronic interaction among Co atom and NCDs, which can provide efficient charge channels for the rapid electron transfer and further improve the intrinsic activity and promote the OER kinetics. The work may endow a new strategy for the construction of excellent transition metal-based hydroxide water oxidation electrocatalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the sharp consumption of non-renewable resources, such as fossil fuel, has become a serious issue all over the globe [1,2]. Seeking environment-friendly and renewable energy substitute is one of paramount routes to address the environment contamination and energy scarcity in the coming years. Be part of oxygen chemistry, water splitting is recognized as a promising clean energy conversion strategy for meeting the above challenges [3,4]. Oxygen evolution reaction (OER) and hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) are two vital half reactions, respectively. However, the higher overpotential and the complicated four electron-proton processes required by OER lead to the bottleneck in the development of water splitting [5]. Up to now, noble metals Ru-, Ir-, and Pt-based composites are regarded as the most efficient electrocatalysts toward OER [6,7]. Nevertheless, the low earth reserves, high cost, and rapid degradation of noble catalysts dramatically hinder their large-scale applications. Consequently, it is imminent to explore fast kinetics, high activity and corrodibility, and earth abundant materials, such as transition metal oxides [8,9], hydroxides [10], sulfides [11], phosphides [12–14], carbide [15], and so on.

To date, first-row (3d) transition metal-based composites, especially cobalt hydroxide-based materials, have been widely investigated as the valid OER catalysts [16]. For example, Arif et al. developed CoFe-layered double hydroxide coupled graphitic carbon nitride composites and it was shown efficient OER performance due to the hierarchical flower-like morphologies [17]. Cui et al. synthesized high dispersed three dimensional (3D) cobalt hydroxide samples gained satisfying water oxidation activity, which was attributed to the special 3D structure that prevent the aggregation of active nanosheets [18]. Thus, the construction of 3D structure is one of efficient avenue for providing large surface area, generating more active sites, and improving the OER activity of cobalt hydroxide-based materials. However, the lack of charge transfer channels and poor electrical conductivity of cobalt hydroxide, which fundamentally leads to the sluggish OER kinetics, should be further accelerated [19].

As one number of carbon-nanomaterial family, carbon dots (CDs) show great potentials for many fields, such as electrocatalysis [20], photocatalysis [21], bioimaging [22], DNA structural transition [23], and chiral assembly [24], owing to their remarkable physical and chemical properties [25,26]. We also notice that CDs play an important role in enhancing the conductivity of NiFe layered double hydroxides [27], CoP [28], MoP [29], and accelerating surface charge transfer of Bi4O5I2 [30]. Further, amidogen modification of CDs (NH2-CDs) has been explored as the active OER catalyst [31]. Generally, the introduce of N species can form new metal-N (Me-N) active site during the synthesis process, resulting in fruitful enhancement of OER [32,33]. What’s more, the negative charge of CDs could attract the positive metal cations, then control the growth of crystal, and finally engineer the structure of composites [34]. Taking the above comprehensive considerations, it is anticipated that after combining of NCDs and cobalt hydroxide, the well-defined structure can be obtained and possess large number of active sites, more charge transfer channels and faster charge transfer, which further boost up the electrochemical water oxidation performance.

In this present work, we fabricated distinctive cobalt chloride carbonate hydroxide coupled with NCDs composites (for briefly we named as Co(OH)2@NCDs) through a facile in situ hydrothermal method for the first time. The shape of materials was efficiently modulated by NCDs. Meanwhile, the effects of the feed mass and the surface charge of NCDs, and the reaction time on the morphology regulation of Co(OH)2@NCDs were systematically investigated. It is manifested that the strong coupling of NCDs not only observably increases the electrochemical active surface area and creates distinct electron transfer channels (Co-N), but modifies the chemical environment of Co center and further optimizes the OER reaction kinetics. The optimized Co(OH)2@NCDs hybrid shows a hierarchical urchin-like structure with large electrochemical active surface area, which is about 29-fold of pristine Co(OH)2. Impressively, it also exhibits the superior electrochemical OER performance (with an overpotential of 296 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 and a Tafel slop of 70.5 mV dec−1) with respect to Co(OH)2 (414 mV, 102.3 mV dec−1) and RuO2 (346 mV, 89 mV dec−1).

Citric acid (99.5%) was purchased from Energy Chemical and ethylenediamine (A.R grade) was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Urea (99%, A.R). Polyethyleneimine (PEI, molecular weight: 1800), folic acid (≥98%) and cobalt chloride hexahydrate (A.R) were acquired from Aladdin Industry Corporation. All of chemicals were used without further purification. Dialysis membranes were purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd and Sangon Biotech (Shanghai) Co., Ltd. Deionized (DI)-water obtained from a Millipore water purification equipment (Merckmillipore, France) was applied to all the experiments.

Negative charge NCDs was synthesized by a facile modified hydrothermal method based on a reported literature [35]. Typically, weighted 20 mmol citric acid precisely were dissolved a mixture solution with 40 ml DI-water and 1.34 ml ethylenediamine. After stirring for 5 min, the mixture solution was transferred into a 100 mlTeflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and then heated at 200 °C for 5 h. After cooling down to room temperature naturally, the dark-brown product was added to water and was then subjected to dialysis (MWCO = 1 KD) for 48 h. To further purify the NCDs, the dialyzed samples were centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 10 min to remove the aggregated particles. Finally, NCDs powers were obtained after freeze drying.

For the synthesis of Co(OH)2 nanowires (NWs), a facile hydrothermal strategy was presented. 3 mmol CoCl2·6H2O and 3 mmol urea were dispersed 20 ml DI-water under ultrasonication for 1 h and then poured into a 50 ml autoclave. After keeping at 100 °C for 12 h, the obtained sample was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min and washed with DI water and ethanol for three times, respectively. Finally, the pink power was achieved after drying at 60 °C for 24 h in a vacuum condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of cobalt hydroxides nitrogen-doped carbon dots composites (Co(OH)2@NCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The procedure to obtain Co(OH)2@NCDs hybrid was similar to the preparation process of Co(OH)2 NWs, except that different amount of fresh NCDs powers were dispersed into the precursor mixed solution before the reaction (Scheme 1 ). The added mass of NCDs was 5, 10, 25, 50, and 100 mg (denoted as Co(OH)2@NCDs-x, x = 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5), respectively. For evidencing the growth mechanism, Co(OH)2@NCDs-4 materials with different reaction times (0.5, 3 and 6 h) were fabricated. In addition, positive charge NCDs (NCDs(+)) were introduced into the synthesis of Co(OH)2@NCDs-4 composite instead of NCDs and named as Co(OH)2@NCDs(+)-4.

UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by a PerkinElmer Lambda 750 spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectra were taken on an Edinburgh FS-5 spectrophotometer equipped with a Xenon lamp. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were performed on a Nicolet iS50 FTIR spectrometer. A home-made Raman system with laser light sources was applied to record room-temperature Raman spectra. The crystal structure of samples was measured by a Bruker-D2 PHASE X-ray diffraction system (Cu kα radiation, λ = 1.5418 Å) and operating in a 2θ range from 10° to 70°. The micromorphology and microstructure of composites were recorded on field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Sigma 500) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-7800F Prime, JEOL and Regulus SU8230, Hitachi). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of materials were recorded on an EMSIS VELETA G3 transmission electron microscope and a JEOL 2100F TEM device at 200 kV. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) image was taken on a MicroNano D-5A scanning probe microscope system (Shanghai Zhuolun MicroNano Equipment Co., Ltd., China). The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area and Barrette-Joynere-Halenda (BJH) pore size of as-prepared samples were obtained by a Quantachrome Autosorb-iQ device, which was carried out at 77 K. Thermogravimetric (TG) curves were recorded on a NETZSCH STA-2500 Regulus and operated in a temperature range of 35–800 °C under air. The zeta potential of the synthesized NCDs was measured by an Anton Paar LITESIZER 500 particle size analyzer at 25 °C ± 1 °C.

In order to prepare the working electrodes, 4 mg of as-made catalyst composite was dispersed in 1 ml of DI-water/ethanol (v/v = 3:2) solution with ultrasonic-assisted for 30 min. Then, 5 μL of Nafion (5 w%) solution was dropped into the above mixture and ultrasonicated for another 30 min. Next, 10 μL of as-prepared catalytic inks were coated on the L-shape glass carbon electrode (GCE, 3 mm in diameter), which was pre-polished by using 0.05 μm aluminum oxide powder, and dried in the air for minutes. The loading of electrocatalysts was ~0.56 mg cm−2. All the electrochemical tests were performed on a standard three-electrode system with a CHI 760E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co., China) at room temperature in 1 M KOH solution (pH = 13.9). A saturated Ag/AgCl electrode was used as the reference electrode, with a Pt wire as the counter electrode. By applying the Nernst equation: ERHE = EAg/AgCl + 0.59 × pH + 0.197, all measured potentials against Ag/AgCl electrode were converted to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE). Before performing all electrochemical characterizations, the electrolyte was purged with N2 for at least 30 min, and all electrocatalysts underwent 25 cycles of cyclic voltammetry method (CV) at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 within the potential window 1.02–1.62 V (vs RHE). Polarization data were obtained at 5 mV s−1 via linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) and with a 95% iR-compensation. High scan-rate CV method was employed to test the cycling durability of the samples at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1 for 2000 cycles. Chronoamperometry (CP) was also used to confirm the long-term stability of the catalysts at controlled overpotential. To determine the electrochemical double layer capacitance (C dl), CV curves within the non-faradaic potential window (1.21–1.31 V versus RHE) at different scan rates of 10–120 mV s−1 were evaluated. Current density (j) was calculated using the following formula j = A/S, where A is the measured current (mA) from CV curves and S is the geometric area of GCE (0.07 cm2). The obtained current density difference (j a-j c) at 1.26 V (vs. RHE) against scan rate has a linear relationship. And C dl was calculated from the linear relationship according to the previous report [36]: i = vC dl, where i represents one-half of the current density difference (j a − j c) at 1.26 V (vs. RHE) and v refers to scan rates. After obtaining C dl, the electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) could be calculated from the following equation: ECSA = C dl/C s, where the C s was regarded as 0.04 mF cm−2 in a 1 M KOH solution [37]. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy curves (EIS) were recorded on a PGSTAT 302N AutoLab electrochemical workstation and measured in a frequency range of 105 Hz-10−1 Hz at an applied potential of 1.52 V (vs. RHE). Turnover frequencies and mass activity value were calculated according to the previous reports [38,39]. With respect to valuing the Faradaic efficiency of the as-prepared electrode, water drainage method was utilized [40].

The NCDs were large-scale synthesized by an optimized bottom-up method as present previously [35]. The AFM and TEM images of as-synthesized NCDs exhibit sphere-like and well-dispersed carbon particles (Fig. S1). The size distribution is illustrated as an inset in Fig. S1b, which proves that the average size of NCDs is almost 2.8 nm. The UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) behaviors of NCDs are displayed in Fig. S2, respectively. For the UV–vis spectrum of NCDs, a wide shoulder peak located at 244 nm and a sharp peak located at 344 nm are attributed to the π-π* and n-π* transitions [20]. Concentrating on the fluorescence spectra, the blue and red chain-dotted line refer to the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths at 339 and 443 nm (Fig. S2a), respectively. Fig. S2b shows that NCDs contain an obvious excitation-dependent behavior, consisting well with the reported fluorescent carbon materials [23,41].

In a typical synthesis of the Co(OH)2-based composites (Scheme 1), Co(OH)2 reveals a nanowire-like morphology without the introduce of NCDs (Fig. 1 a) and the growth mechanism has investigated by previous report in detail [42]. After adding different quantities of NCDs, 3D Co(OH)2@NCDs materials with different morphologies are obtained by a hydrothermal reaction at 100 °C for 12 h. SEM was performed to investigate the effect of NCDs feed mass on the structure of Co(OH)2@NCDs composites. As shown in Fig. 1b and 1c, with a low content of NCDs feed mass, double bell-like nanomaterials could be obtained. With the increased quantity of NCDs (25 mg), Co(OH)2@NCDs-3 sample with both double-bell and slack urchin-like shape is achieved (Fig. 1d). After the additional amount of NCDs up to 50 mg, the well-defined 3D echinus-like structures with a diameter of ~1.5 μm are emerged (Fig. 1e). Generally, the open and uniform structure, with large specific area, provides more opportunity for active sites getting in touch with electrolyte. However, a further increased quantity of NCDs up to 100 mg in the precursor can change the sea urchin-like materials into microspheres and make nanoneedles anchor densely, which reduces the exposed active sites (Fig. 1f). According to the above analysis, it can be noted that the NCDs can efficiently regulate the morphologies of Co(OH)2@NCDs composites. TG measurement was utilized to evaluate the true amounts of NCDs in various Co(OH)2@NCDs samples. As illustrated in Fig. S3, it shows a sharp loss of weight in all Co(OH)2@NCDs around 227 °C, which is ascribed to the removal of structure water and carbon dioxide. The weight loss corresponding to Co(OH)2@NCDs-1, Co(OH)2@NCDs-2, Co(OH)2@NCDs-3, Co(OH)2@NCDs-4, and Co(OH)2@NCDs-5 are 1.03%, 1.77%, 4.68%, 6.29%, and 13.32% after subtracting the weight loss of Co(OH)2, confirming the measured weight loss are agree well with the feeding amounts.

The structure and morphology of the as-prepared Co(OH)2@NCDs-4 electrocatalyst were characterized by SEM, TEM, as well as high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). High-magnification SEM and TEM images show that nanoneedles uniformly anchored on the surface of microspheres (Fig. 2 a–c). HRTEM image clearly displays the successful combination between the Co(OH)2 and the NCDs (Fig. 2d). According to the standard card of Co(CO3)0.35Cl0.20(OH)1.10 NWs (JCPDS: 38-0547), the lattice fringes of 0.25 and 0.235 nm are attributed to the XRD peaks corresponding to 36.1° and 39.4° [32,42]. The inter-planar distance of 0.21 nm is ascribed to (100) plane of NCDs [43–45]. The selected area electronic diffraction (SAED) (Fig. 2d1) shows a polycrystalline pattern, demonstrating the formation of coupled Co(OH)2@NCDs-4 structures. Additionally, overlap and element mapping images reveal the homogenously distribution of cobalt, oxygen, carbon, nitrogen on the surface of Co(OH)2@NCDs-4 composite (Fig. 2e-j). Nitrogen absorption-desorption measurements were further carried out to investigate the porous structure of Co(OH)2@NCDs-4. As shown in Fig. S4, by employing BET analysis method, the geometric specific surface area (GMSA) of Co(OH)2@NCDs-4 is measured to 19.4 m2 g−1, which is more than about 2 times as high as that of pure Co(OH)2 (10.5 m2 g−1). The main pore size distribution of Co(OH)2@NCDs-4 is 1.66 nm, which is determined by BJH method. The enhanced surface area and open structure of Co(OH)2@NCDs-4 will not only remarkably increase the number of active sites, but provide more diffusion channels for mass transport and charge transfer, which further promote the electrochemical water oxidation activity.

For further investigating the growth mechanism of urchin-like Co(OH)2@NCDs-4, hydrothermal reaction with different time intervals (0.5, 3, 6 h) were firstly carried out. When the mixed precursors were heated in a short time (0.5 h), no any characteristic refraction peaks could be observed in the hybrid (Fig. 3 a) and only nanoplates with different sizes emerged (Fig. 3b). After prolonging the reaction time (3 h and 6 h), numerous new XRD peaks in accord well with Co(CO3)0.35Cl0.20(OH)1.10 NWs (JCPDS: 38-0547) crystal appeared [32,42]. Correspondingly, nanoplates transformed to urchin-like structures where nanoneedles being with a radioactive distribution pattern, covering the surface of microspheres (Fig. 3c and d). At the same time, Co(OH)2@NCDs(+)-4 hybrid was synthesized by adding positive charge NCDs (NCDs(+)) during the synthesis procedure instead of NCDs to figure out the role of surface charge of NCDs in morphology control. The zeta potential value of NCDs(+) was measured as +11.96 mV, which was in stark contrast to the −3.77 mV of NCDs (Fig. S5). As illustrated in Fig. S6, the different magnification SEM images of Co(OH)2@NCDs(+)-4 show that a large number of nanowires are tied together instead of assembling into a sea urchin structure, meaning the NCDs(+) cannot modify the morphology of Co(OH)2. Therefore, the proper growth mechanism of Co(OH)2@NCDs may be presented as follows: In the cobalt precursor solution, after adding NCDs, the negative NCDs firstly interact with cobalt cations by electrostatic interaction via functional group. Urea slowly pyrolyzes to generate OH anion and carbonate with the increasing of temperature. Meanwhile, cobalt cations bind with OH anion to form cobalt hydroxide hydrate@NCDs nanoparticles. Then, the nanoparticles start to grow up to form nanosheets and anions (Cl− and CO3 2−) intercalate into the interlayer. Owing to the multi-functional groups on the surface of NCDs and the electrostatic interaction, dozens of favorable sites on the nanosheets drive the growth of crystal [46]. For decreasing the surface energy, nanosheets decreased the scale, arranged well and then stacked together to assemble into one-dimensional nanoneedles. Finally, nanoneedles uniformly cover the nanoplates and form the 3D echinus-like structure.

A series of characterization measurements for exactly exploring the interactions between Co(OH)2 and NCDs were further carried out. From XRD pattern (Fig. 4 a and Fig. S7a), a broad XRD peak located at 23° corresponds to highly disordered carbon atom in NCDs sample [35]. The sharp reflections peaks at 17.4°, 33.4°, 34.9°, 36.4°, 39.1°, 46.5°, 53.3°, 55.4°, and 61.3° match well with the standard card (JCPDS: 38-0547) of Co(CO3)0.35Cl0.20(OH)1.10 in Co(OH)2 and Co(OH)2@NCDs samples [32,42]. However, no obvious peaks at around 23° can be observed in Co(OH)2@NCDs, which may due to the low amount of NCDs contained in the Co(OH)2@NCDs composite. FT-IR spectra of the as-prepared samples are displayed in Fig. 4b. The peak at approximately 3440 cm−1 and 2937 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of O–H and N–H bands. The peak located at 1698 cm−1 is assigned to vibrational absorption of CO band. The asymmetric stretching vibration peak located at 1187 cm−1 is ascribed to the C–NH–C [35]. FT-IR results give a fruitful evidence that NCDs possess multi-functional groups, which can be effectively used as nuclei sites donor for fabrication electrocatalysts [33,46]. With regard to the FT-IR curves of Co(OH)2@NCDs (Fig. 4b and Fig. S7b), they are similar to Co(OH)2 except two new peaks emerge at 1696 and 1650 cm−1, which is respectively assigned to the vibration and bending vibration of CO and N–H. The peaks located at 830.8 cm−1, 947.8 cm−1, 1044.3 cm−1, and 1075.3 cm−1 within the blue region can be attributed to the vibration of CO3 2−, indicating the existence of inserted CO3 2− [47]. Additionally, the decreased intensity of C–NH–C can be seen in Co(OH)2@NCDs-4, suggesting the strong interactions between Co(OH)2 and NCDs. In the far infrared region, the peak at 519 cm−1 is identified to the Co-O vibrations. Raman results are illustrated in Fig. 4c. It is clearly shown that the typical D band (1358 cm−1) and G band (1602 cm−1) exist in the Co(OH)2@NCDs-4 spectrum, confirming the exitance of graphitic carbon, which is consistent with XPS analysis. Another band located at 676.3 cm−1 is indicative of Co(OH)2 [48,49]. Moreover, the PL spectra of NCDs obviously quenches after combining with Co(OH)2 (Fig. 4d), nicely demonstrating the electron transfer between NCDs and Co(OH)2.

XPS was conducted to further confirm the elements chemical environment of catalysts and the coupling effect between NCDs and Co(OH)2. As displayed in Fig. 5 a, the survey shows the main elements of Co, O, N, and C in Co(OH)2@NCDs-4. No extra N species is introduced into the synthesis procedure, thus no signal of N element around 400 eV that can be found in the full-scan XPS spectrum of pristine Co(OH)2. As for the C 1s spectrum of Co(OH)2 (Fig. S8a), three peaks are attributed to C–C, C–O, and O–CO, respectively [50]. It is clearly found that three peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.15 eV, and 287.9 eV in both NCDs and Co(OH)2@NCDs-4 composites, which are assigned to C–C/CC, C–N, and C–O, respectively [51]. Compared with NCDs, a peak located at 289.2 eV with relative high content in the C 1s of Co(OH)2@NCDs-4, is ascribed to N–CO [51]. As shown in Fig. 5c, four peaks are well fitted at 399 eV (pyridinic N), 399.9 eV (Co-N/pyrrolic N), 400.7 eV (graphitic N), and 404.2 eV (oxidized N) in the N 1s spectrum of Co(OH)2@NCDs-4 [51,52]. Apparently, the distinct peak at 399.9 eV corresponding to Co–N in Co(OH)2@NCDs-4 and the shift of the peak are as a proof concept, indicating the coupled interactions between NCDs and Co(OH)2 [33]. The high-resolution O 1s spectrum (Fig. S8b) of Co(OH)2@NCDs-4 reveals the presence of Co-O (530.3 eV), OH group and C–O (531.3 eV), as well as CO bonds (532.1 eV) [38,53]. With respect to the XPS spectrum of Co 2p, the peak located at 782.4 eV, which fitted in Co(OH)2@NCDs-4, is assigned to the Co–N bond, demonstrating that Co species can couple with NCDs by a coordination interaction and then generate Co–N charge transfer channel (Fig. 5d) [54]. While other peak deconvoluted at 780.8 eV is attributed to Co2+ of Co(OH)2 [55]. Notably, the EDX element mapping shows that the signals of Co and N elements are highly overlaid with each other in the Co(OH)2@NCDs-4, which is further believed to be Co-N [56–59]. The high-resolution Cl 2p spectra of Co(OH)2 and Co(OH)2@NCDs-4 are presented in Fig. 5e, confirming the existence of Cl−. The spin-orbit doublets at 198.1 eV and 199.7 eV for Cl 2p of Co(OH)2@NCDs-4 can be attributed to Cl 2p3/2 and Cl 2p1/2, respectively [42]. Importantly, the peak location for Co 2p, N 1s, and Cl 2p are shifted in Co(OH)2@NCDs-4 compared with Co(OH)2 and NCDs, suggesting that the formation of Co-N induced by the coupling of NCDs with Co(OH)2 can facilitate the charge transfer, leading to the optimized electronic structure [32,33,51,60,61]. All above characterizations give a powerful evidence for the successfully obtained of hierarchical Co(OH)2@NCDs-4 hybrid, and it is expected that the electrochemical activity of Co(OH)2@NCDs-4 hybrid can be significantly enhanced.

Afterwards, the electrochemical performances of Co(OH)2@NCDs composites toward OER were comprehensively studied by a standard three-electrode system in N2-saturated 1 M KOH media at room temperature. For comparison, we also tested the OER property of commercial RuO2 electrocatalyst. All electrocatalysts were firstly dispersed onto the GCE and stabilized in electrolyte by CV method before LSV measurement. Up to now, many works reported that NCDs exhibited poor electrocatalytic OER performance [46,51]. However, LSV curves suggest that the obtained Co(OH)2@NCDs catalysts display a better OER activity than the pure Co(OH)2 (Fig. 6 a). Notably, except Co(OH)2, all Co(OH)2@NCDs catalysts present an oxidation peak at ~1.15 V (vs. RHE), which is attributed to the Co3+/Co2+ redox process [62]. After coupling with NCDs, the available cobalt ions in Co(OH)2@NCDs are activated and the oxidation currents are increased, which promote the OER process at high potentials [39]. When the feed mass of NCDs is controlled at 50 mg, Co(OH)2@NCDs-4 with highest oxidation peak reveals current densities (j) of 10 and 50 mA cm−2 at 296 and 352 mV, which are markedly lower than those of Co(OH)2 (414 and 566 mV), Co(OH)2@NCDs-1 (367 and 430 mV), Co(OH)2@NCDs-2 (331 and 385 mV), Co(OH)2@NCDs-3 (318 and 365 mV), Co(OH)2@NCDs-5 (316 and 377 mV), and RuO2 catalyst (346 and 470 mV), respectively. In addition, Co(OH)2@NCDs-4 exhibits an expected lower Tafel slope (70.5 mV dec−1) than Co(OH)2 (102.3 mV dec−1) and other samples (Fig. 6d), suggesting its improved OER reaction kinetics. With respect to physical mixture of Co(OH)2 and NCDs (Co(OH)2/NCDs-4), an increased overpotential and Tafel slope are observed in Fig. S9, which further proves the strong synergic interactions between Co(OH)2 and NCDs. CV method was then used to determine the electrochemical double-layer capacitance (C dl) (Fig. S10), and the electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) was calculated based on the C dl [37]. As shown in Fig. 6e, the estimated C dl of the Co(OH)2@NCDs-4 sample (51.48 mF cm−2) is larger than those of Co(OH)2 (1.77 mF cm−2), Co(OH)2@NCDs-1 (4.02 mF cm−2), Co(OH)2@NCDs-2 (5.73 mF cm−2), Co(OH)2@NCDs-3 (28.17 mF cm−2), Co(OH)2@NCDs-5 (39.8 mF cm−2), RuO2 (4.92 mF cm−2), and NCDs (0.41 mF cm−2) electrocatalysts, respectively. As expected, the Co(OH)2@NCDs-4 catalyst owns the large ECSA value, indicating that Co(OH)2@NCDs-4 possesses high effective electrocatalytic active area for OER (Table S1). Turnover frequencies (TOFs) and mass activity (MA) were calculated at an overpotential of 350 mV and were then applied to elucidate the intrinsic activity of these electrocatalysts. Generally, all Co atoms are assumed as active sites. Notably, the MA in Co(OH)2@NCDs-4 reaches values as high as 83.9 A g−1, which is approximately 25.4 and 4.4 times higher than those of Co(OH)2 (3.3 A g−1) and the RuO2 (19.2 A g−1) (Fig. S11a). Briefly, the TOFs value of Co(OH)2@NCDs-4 (0.432 s−1) is about 39 times higher than that of Co(OH)2 (0.011 s−1) and signally higher than those of Co(OH)2@NCDs-1 (0.045 s−1), Co(OH)2@NCDs-2 (0.176 s−1), Co(OH)2@NCDs-3 (0.293 s−1), Co(OH)2@NCDs-5 (0.242 s−1) (Fig. S11b), manifesting that coupling with NCDs significantly influences the electronic environment of Co sites, thus improves the intrinsic performance of active Co sites. Moreover, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was employed to deeply analysis the reaction kinetics. Nyquist plots were recorded and well simulated on an AutoLab electrochemical workstation by the equivalent circuit model (Fig. 6f and Fig. S12a). Equivalent circuit fitted curves show a similar low solution resistance (Rs), revealing the great conductivity of electrolyte. The semicircles of curves refer to the charge-transfer resistance (Rct) and the corresponding value is utilized to analyze the OER reaction kinetics [33,63–65]. As is expected, excessive addition of NCDs decreases the Rct of Co(OH)2@NCDs. In particular, the Co(OH)2@NCDs-4 shows a remarkably smaller Rct than other catalysts (Fig. S12b), evidencing that it processes faster reaction kinetics. The above electrochemical characterization results impressively prove that the incorporation of NCDs can modify the electrocatalytic kinetics and enhance the intrinsic electrocatalytic activity of Co(OH)2@NCDs. Nevertheless, an excessive increase in NCDs degrades the performance. This may be caused by the dense accumulation of nanoneedles, which reduces the active sites and limits the interaction between electrolyte and electrocatalysts. Water drainage method was used to measure the Faradaic efficiency (FE) during the OER process by a set of home-made H-type electrolytic cell (Fig. S13a) [40]. As presented in Fig. S13b, the amount of measured O2 is in line with the theoretical value, suggesting the nearly 100% FE of Co(OH)2@NCDs-4 toward OER. Finally, in comparison to several similar samples reported recently, the OER activity of Co(OH)2@NCDs-4 is can be considered as an excellent electrocatalyst (Table S2).

In order to deeply cognized the electrocatalytic mechanism, crystal structure, morphology, chemical and valance state of Co(OH)2@NCDs-4 before and after OER stability test were traced by XRD, SEM and XPS. There is an obvious structure and morphology change after long-term stability measurement (Fig. S14). Besides the diffraction peaks of Co(CO3)0.35Cl0.20(OH)1.10, the new generated peaks at 11.9°, 23.9° and 47.3° are assigned to (00n) plane of α-Co(OH)2 ((JCPDS: 46-0605) and the other peaks at 37.6°, 45.9°, 50.6°, 62.1° and 65.3° are ascribed to (101), (104), (015), (107) and (110) plane of CoOOH (JCPDS: 73-0497), respectively. What’s more, nanoneedles are dissolved after long-term OER test (Fig. S14b). Many plates with different thicknesses are loosely covering on the surface of spheres and even some micro-blocks produced. It suggests that OH− replaces the inserted anions (CO3 2− and Cl−) via an ion-exchange process, then nanoneedles transform to plates and randomly assemble into α-Co(OH)2 plates through the dissolution-recrystallization, and finally Co2+ oxidizes to Co3+ and converts into the CoOOH under alkaline electrolyte [66,67]. From the XPS spectra, peaks of Co 2p, N 1s and O 1s all shift to low binding energy after OER (Fig. S15). Furthermore, the intensity of Co-N at both Co 2p and N 1s dramatically decreases and a sharp peak at 780 eV is observed at Co 2p spectra, evidencing that Co2+ evolving into high valance state of cobalt species [49,68,69]. The intensity of Co-O separated at 530.5 eV shows a markable increase further illustrating the formation of CoOOH [70]. Overall, under the alkaline OER condition, the specific sea urchin-like structure of Co(OH)2@NCDs-4 has changed. However, it shows a high stable catalytic activity which is confirmed by a mild increased of the applied potential after 20,000 s long-term test compared with RuO2 (Fig. 7 a) and the negligible degeneration after 2000 CV cycling measurements (Fig. 7b and Fig. S16), respectively. Besides, the rough-surface morphology is beneficial to the mass transport and the diffusion of electrolyte, which is responsible for the high activity and stability of OER [71]. Based on the above XRD, SEM and XPS analyses, the excellent catalytic performance of Co(OH)2@NCDs-4 can be assigned to the transformed CoOOH, which is regarded as the active site for OER [72,73]. To further understand the role of the NCDs in enhancing the OER performance, the most classical Krasil’shchikov path of the electrically driven OER in alkaline media as shown below [5]:

ce:formula.ce:chem: M* + OH− → M*OH + e− (120 mV dec−1)
M*OH + OH− → M*O− + H2O (60 mV dec−1)

ce:section.ce:para: The Tafel slope is an indicator to determine the rate-determining step (RDS) of metal hydroxide based electrocatalysts in OER system [74,75]. Fig. 6d shows that the Tafel slope of Co(OH)2 and Co(OH)2@NCDs-4 are 102.3 mV dec−1 and 70.5 mV dec−1, respectively. Clearly, the RDS of Co(OH)2 is assigned to a step involving the first electron transfer step, whereas the RDS of Co(OH)2@NCDs-4 is diagnostic of a step involving a chemical transformation following the reaction (1) [76,77]. Meanwhile, these results evidence that the NCDs coupling can efficiently facilitate the transformation of M*OH to M*O− on the surface of active sites and further optimized the OER kinetics [78].

From the above, all characterizations clearly confirmed that the Co(OH)2@NCDs hybrid revealed the outstanding OER performance and the reasons were as follows. Firstly, by rational controlling the feed mass and the type of surface charge, NCDs could regulate the morphologies of Co(OH)2@NCDs to form 3D urchin-like structures with large electrochemical active surface area, which increased the density of active sites. Secondly, multi-functional groups on the surface of NCDs made a strong association between NCDs with Co(OH)2, which provided numerous channels to facilitate charge transfer from NCDs to Co(OH)2. Thirdly, the small size of conductive NCDs exhibited the electron donors/acceptors property, increasing the conductivity of Co(OH)2. Finally, the new generated Co-N and the modified cobalt active sites in Co(OH)2@NCDs, as active centers, optimized the OER kinetics, enhanced the intrinsic activity, and promoted the reaction.

In summary, we have confirmed the successful fabrication of 3D Co(OH)2@NCDs OER electrocatalyst via a facile in situ hydrothermal method with the assistance of NCDs. The feed mass and the type of surface charge of NCDs were found to regulate the morphology of Co(OH)2@NCDs nanomaterials. The optimized urchin-like Co(OH)2@NCDs-4 composite shows not only impressive OER activity but also high durability, which is superior to Co(OH)2 and RuO2. The coupling of NCDs constructed the well-defined 3D structure, increasing the active sites. At the same time, the smooth charge transfer channel (e. g., Co-N) was generated and the electronic structure of Co active site was modified, leading to the high intrinsic electrochemical activity and the fast OER kinetics of Co(OH)2@NCDs. Our study highlights the synergic effects originated from NCDs in morphology modulation, intermediates dissociation kinetics, and intrinsic activity improvement, and provides a novel design avenue for developing highly efficient and low-cost electrocatalyst toward the OER.
